California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
September 19, 1990
Honorable Ross Johnson
Assembtymember, 64th Assembly District
State capitol
P. O. Box 942449
sacramento, CA 94249-oooL
Re: Your Request for Advice
our File No. r-9 o-2 5 3
Dear Assembl]rmenber Johnson:
This is in response to your reguest for confirmation of
telephone advice provided to you by this office concerning the
propriety of using canpaign funds to pay for generic-voter
registralion and absentee ballot programs by a political party
un6er the carnpaj-qn provisions of the Political Reform Act (the
nasgrr1.1/ tnL iisue of which expenditures are considered
associated with a candidate's office was considered at the July
Commission meeting which delayed issuance of this letter. 
since
your advice request seeks general guidance with respect to your
iuestion, we are treating your request as one for infornal as-
Ji=t".,"" . 2 /
OUESTIONS
1. May an elected official or candidate contribute cautpaign
funds to a political party for greneric voter registration activity
which is delignated for a district other than the of f j'cial's 
oun?
2. May an elected official 
or candj.date contribute carnpaign
funds to a political party for absentee ballot programs which are
designated for a district other than the official's 
own?
L/ covernment code sections 81ooo-9L015. A11 statutory refer-
ences are to the Government code unless otherwise indi-cated. com-
mission regulatj-ons appear at 2 california code of Regulations
section I-8OOO, et seo. A]1 references to regulations are to
Title 2, Division 6 of the California code of Regulations. Sec-
tj-ons 858o0-85807, which describe the perrnissible uses of carnpaign
funds, are referred to as the rrpersonal userr la$/s.
2/ rnformal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
irnnunity provided by an opinion or formal wrj-tten advice. (Section
83114; Regulation 18329 (c) (3) . )
428 J Street, Suite 8OO o P.O. Box 8O7 o Sacramento CA 958O4-O8O7 . (916)322-5660


Our File No. 
■_‐'0-253
Page 2
CONCLUSIONS
1. An elected off j.cial or candidate may not contribute
campaiqn funds to a political party for generic voter. registration
actlvity which is specifically designated for a district 
other
than the official's 
own. However, canpaj-gn funds may be
contributed to a political party for generic voter reqistration
activity in the official's 
district or statewide.
2. An elected officiat or candidate may not contribute
canpaign funds to a political partY for absentee ballot programs
wfritn ire specifically designated for a district 
other than the
official's 
own. However, canpaign funds may be contributed to a
political party for absentee ba11ot prograns in the official's
district 
or statewide.
DISCUSSION
This letter confirms that your letter dated March 27 , 1990,
accurately sumrnarizes the Commission's current position on the use
of canpaign funds for voter registration. 
Section 85201(c)
requir-es 6andidates for public office to deposit all carnpaign
co;tributions they receive into a carnpaign bank account.
Section 85202 (b) states:
A11 contributions deposited into the campaign
account sha11 be deemed to be held in trust for
expenses associated with the election of the
candidate to the specific office for which the
candidate has stated, pursuant to Section 85200,
that he or she intends to seek or expenses associ-
ated rrith holding that office.
Sections 85201(c) and 85202(b) read together require that any
expenditure of canpaign funds be associated with a candidate's
cairpaign for the office for vhich the funds were raised.
negistering voters of a candidate's party affiLiation 
j-n the
caididate'i jurisdiction and encouraging voting through absentee
ballot prograns are clearly related to the candidate's ability to
seek and hold office. 
Thus, contributing canpaign funds to a
political party or political 
cornmittee for these purposes is
^consistent- witi the trust.3/ 
(uartin Advice Letter, No. A-89-301,
J t 
payments for voter
candidate's account for
copy enclosed. )
registration hust be made fron the
a future campaign. (Regulation La525,


Our Fi■e No. ■-90■253
Page 3
copy encLosea.l'l
Hoerever, the Conmission has consistently interpreted the
trust provision as precluding a candidate frorn using campaign
funds for communications which predorninantly benefit other
candj.dates. (Milnan Advice letter, No. I-90-206.) Consequently,
a candidate may not nake q4 expenditure for voter registration
outside his jurisdiction.5/ 
This linitation 
is consistent with
Section 85304, which prohibits candidates from transferring
campaign funds to other candidates, and other corunj.ssion advice
concerning the use of canpaign funds. (Dowell Advice Letter,
No. A-89-566, copy enclosed. )
If you have any further questions regarding this natter,
please feel free to contact ne at (916) 322-59OL.
Sincerely,
Scott HaIlabrin
Acting ceneral counsel
一
ohn w.
wa I lace
Legal Division
unse1,
SH:」WW:p■h
Enc■osures
4/ rn reaching this conclusion, the cornmission staff is cognizant
of Regulation 18535 (copy enclosed). This reg"ulation sets forth
the reguireruents under which political parties and political
action -cornmittees nay receive contributions not subject to the
Act's contribution linits. 
(Sections 85302 and 85303(c).) Under
Section 85304 and Commission advice consistent with this section,
we do not interpret this regulation to permit candidates to
earmark and transfer canpaign funds as described above- In
reaching this conclusion, the Conrnissj.on staff is cognizant of our
advice in the Martin Advice Letter, No. A-89-301 (copy enclosed)
in which we advised that a candidate can transfer funds in
untimited amounts to a political party as long as the fund were to
be used rrfor purposes other than naking contributions to
candidates.r' We indicated in that letter that the Commission had
not yet defined the phrase 'rfor purposes other than naking
contiibutions to candidates.!t Thus, we see no conflict between
our conclusions in the Martin letter and this letter. 
However, to
the extent that this advice letter conflicts with the Martin
Advice Letter, the current Letter controls,
5/ I{here a contribution is made to a state party organization for
a statewide program of voter registration, we would presume the
prirnary benefit of the candidate's contribution is obtained by the
candidate absent facts to the contrary.
